original_hypothesis.title: AVM-Delegated veNFT Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked in AutoVotingEscrow Contracts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bribe.getReward() sends rewards to ownerOf(tokenId) which returns the AutoVotingEscrow (AVE) instance address for AVM-managed tokens. The check `_owner == avm` compares the AVE address against the AutoVotingEscrowManager address — always false. Rewards are transferred to the AVE contract which has zero ERC20 recovery functions. Additionally, setOriginalOwner in the manager is a no-op (empty body), and VotingEscrow._transferFrom's `_to == avm` check never triggers because the actual transfer destination is the AVE instance, not the manager. All bribe rewards for AVM-managed tokens are permanently lost.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a double-layered integration failure affecting every user who enables auto-voting. The AVE address != AVM manager address comparison guarantees the wrong code path. Even if patched, the no-op setOriginalOwner provides no fallback.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOf returns AVE address; `_owner == avm` compares AVE vs Manager (always false); safeTransfer sends to AVE with no recovery

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting for at least one veNFT
The AVM-managed veNFT has voted for pools with bribe rewards
gaugeManager calls getReward for the tokenId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: AutoVotingEscrow holds NFTs but has no ERC20 withdrawal. Bribe.getReward sends tokens to ownerOf which is the AVE.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: setOriginalOwner is empty (line ~248 in AutoVotingEscrowManager); no admin action can fix already-locked funds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — every bribe claim for AVM tokens triggers this loss

operational_questions: How many tokens are currently managed by AVM instances?
Have any bribe getReward calls already been made for AVM-managed tokens?
validation_requirements: Verify ownerOf(tokenId) returns AVE address, not AVM manager address
Confirm AutoVotingEscrow has no ERC20 transfer function
Verify setOriginalOwner function body is empty

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Any user calls enableAutoVoting(tokenId) — normal protocol usage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: AVM executor votes the token via performLockAction (normal operation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: gaugeManager calls getReward — rewards sent to AVE contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens are permanently locked in AVE with no recovery path
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this occurs during normal protocol operation

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All bribe rewards for AVM-delegated tokens are permanently lost
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: AVM feature becomes a value trap — users lose all bribe income
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without contract upgrade; locked funds in immutable AVE contracts
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All bribe reward distribution for AVM-managed veNFTs

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None required — this is a systemic bug, not an exploit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC20 balances accumulating in AutoVotingEscrow contracts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users enabling auto-voting stop receiving bribe rewards

validation_result.mechanism: AVM address mismatch causes bribe rewards to be permanently locked in AVE contracts
validation_result.impact_summary: All bribe rewards for AVM-managed veNFTs are permanently lost. Rewards are sent to AutoVotingEscrow contract instances that have no ERC20 recovery function.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis describes a multi-layered integration failure that is fully confirmed by code analysis. The chain of failures:

1. When enableAutoVoting is called, the veNFT is transferred to an AutoVotingEscrow (AVE) instance contract, NOT the AutoVotingEscrowManager (AVM manager). ownerOf(tokenId) returns the AVE instance address.

2. In VotingEscrow._transferFrom, the check `_to == avm` compares the transfer destination (AVE instance address) against `avm` (the AVM manager address). These are different contracts, so the condition is always false, and setOriginalOwner is never called.

3. Even if setOriginalOwner were called, it has an empty body - it's a no-op function.

4. In Bribe.getReward, the check `_owner == avm` compares the AVE instance address (from ownerOf) against the AVM manager address. This is always false, so the code never attempts to resolve the original owner.

5. Rewards are sent via safeTransfer to the AVE instance address. The AutoVotingEscrow contract has no ERC20 transfer or recovery function.

6. The AVM manager's getOriginalOwner function DOES properly look up the original owner via avm.lockOwner(tokenId), but this function is never called by Bribe.getReward - it uses originalOwner(tokenId) which reads from a never-populated mapping.

This results in permanent, irrecoverable loss of all bribe rewards for every AVM-delegated veNFT.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms every claim in the hypothesis. The proof-of-signal lines in Bribes.sol match the described pattern. The empty setOriginalOwner function body is visible. The address mismatch between AVE instances and AVM manager is architectural and cannot be worked around.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: ownerOf returns AVE instance address. avm is the AVM manager address. The check _owner == avm is always false for AVM-managed tokens. Rewards are sent to the AVE contract which has no ERC20 recovery.

The condition _to == avm compares the transfer destination (AVE instance) against avm (the manager). Since enableAutoVoting transfers to address(target) where target is the AVE instance, this condition is always false. setOriginalOwner is never called.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: function setOriginalOwner(uint256 _tokenId, address _user) external onlyVotingEscrow {} // implement this
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Empty function body. Even if called, it would not populate the originalOwner mapping. The comment '// implement this' confirms this is an unfinished implementation.

Token is transferred to address(target) which is the AVE instance, not the manager. This means ownerOf(tokenId) will return the AVE address, not the manager address, breaking the identity check in Bribe.getReward.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls enableAutoVoting(tokenId) on AVM manager
3. votingEscrow.transferFrom(msg.sender, address(target), tokenId) - NFT goes to AVE instance
4. VotingEscrow._transferFrom checks _to == avm: address(AVE_instance) == address(AVM_manager) → FALSE
5. setOriginalOwner is never called (and is a no-op anyway)
6. Later: executor calls performLockAction → AVE.voteLocks → IVoter.vote - votes placed for AVM token
8. Bribe.getReward: _owner = ownerOf(tokenId) → returns AVE instance address
10. safeTransfer sends reward tokens to AVE instance contract
11. Tokens are permanently locked - AVE has no ERC20 recovery function
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is in Bribe.getReward where the owner resolution fails. ownerOf(tokenId) returns the AVE instance address (set in step 3). The Bribe's avm variable is set in the constructor to IVotingEscrow(ve).avm() which returns the AVM manager address (VotingEscrow.sol avm state variable). These are fundamentally different addresses - one is the manager contract, the other is one of potentially many child AVE contracts. The comparison _owner == avm can never be true for AVM-managed tokens.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for the AVM system setup. The Bribe constructor sets avm from IVotingEscrow(ve).avm() and VotingEscrow sets avm in the constructor. This is hardcoded architectural behavior, not configuration-dependent.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario but a systemic bug. Any user who calls enableAutoVoting for their veNFT will have bribe rewards permanently lost when getReward is called. The sequence is: (1) User calls enableAutoVoting - normal usage. (2) Executor calls performLockAction to vote - normal operation. (3) gaugeManager calls getReward - rewards sent to AVE contract with no recovery. No special timing, coordination, or attacker action needed. This occurs during completely normal protocol operation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL - Every AVM-managed veNFT permanently loses all bribe rewards. This affects all users who enable auto-voting. The total value at risk equals all bribe rewards allocated to AVM-managed tokens across all epochs. There is no recovery mechanism: the AVE contract has no ERC20 withdrawal function, and the tokens cannot be retrieved without a contract upgrade. Additionally, the RewardsDistributor.claim_many function has a similar issue for expired locks - it sends directly to ownerOf without AVM resolution.
recommended_mitigations: Implement setOriginalOwner to actually store the original owner: originalOwner[_tokenId] = _user;

Fix the address comparison in Bribe.getReward to check if the owner is an AVE instance rather than comparing against the manager address. Consider using tokenIdToAVMId mapping: if(IAutoVotingEscrowManager(avm).tokenIdToAVMId(tokenId) != 0) { _owner = IAutoVotingEscrowManager(avm).getOriginalOwner(tokenId); }
Fix VotingEscrow._transferFrom to detect AVE instances or remove the _to == avm check since the transfer destination is never the manager
Add an ERC20 recovery function to AutoVotingEscrow as a safety net, callable only by the manager
Fix RewardsDistributor.claim_many to include the same AVM owner resolution logic as claim()
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow with avm set to AVM manager address. 2. Deploy AVM manager and create an AVE instance. 3. Deploy Bribe contract (avm set from VE.avm()). 4. Create a veNFT and enable auto-voting - verify ownerOf returns AVE address, not manager address. 5. Add bribe rewards via notifyRewardAmount. 6. Vote with AVM token. 7. Call getReward - verify reward tokens are sent to AVE contract. 8. Verify AVE contract has no function to recover the tokens. 9. Check that originalOwner mapping returns address(0) for the token.
uncertainties: The exact frequency and total value of bribe rewards for AVM-managed tokens in production is unknown
It is unclear whether getReward has already been called for any AVM-managed tokens in production (if deployed)
additional_testing_needed: Integration test confirming the full flow from enableAutoVoting through getReward to demonstrate funds locked in AVE
Check if any deployed instances already have tokens stuck in AVE contracts
Verify RewardsDistributor.claim_many has the same issue for expired AVM locks
